trm:term.trm:name: Reading Map
trm:term.trm:definition: A curated sequence of spec nodes arranged as a linear reading path for a specific audience. Reading maps compose the spec's graph structure into guided entry points without altering the underlying node relationships.

trm:term.trm:name: Topic Reference
trm:term.trm:definition: An entry in a reading map that points to a spec node by ID. Topicrefs can be marked as required (mandatory reading) or optional (supplementary material), and can nest to form sub-sections within a reading path.

trm:term.trm:name: Profiling
trm:term.trm:definition: Metadata attributes on reading map elements that identify the intended audience, experience level, or role. Profiling is inclusive by default: absence of an attribute means the content applies to everyone. Filtering based on profiling values is a tooling concern.

pr:section.pr:title: Reading Maps
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: Composing the spec's graph structure into guided reading sequences for specific audiences.
pr:section.pr:p: A specification's content forms a graph: nodes reference each other through relations, terminology links, and cross-references. This structure is powerful for precision but offers no guided entry point. A newcomer cannot simply "start reading" without knowing which nodes matter and in what order.

A reading map solves this by composing nodes into a linear sequence. Each map has a title, optional parts for hierarchical grouping, and topic references that point to existing nodes by ID. The map does not duplicate content: it provides an ordering overlay on the existing graph.

Maps support profiling attributes: audience, experience-level, and role. These are metadata only and inclusive by default. A topicref with audience="developer" is relevant to developers but not excluded from other audiences unless tooling applies a filter.
pr:section.pr:note: Reading maps are part of the organization layer because they classify how content is navigated, complementing topic typing (concept, task, reference) which classifies what content is.

pr:section.pr:title: Map Structure
pr:section.pr:shortdesc: How maps, parts, and topicrefs compose into a reading path.
pr:section.pr:p: An org:map is the top-level container. It requires an ID (for cross-referencing) and a title. Its children are either parts (for grouping) or topicrefs (for direct node references). Parts contain a title and one or more topicrefs.

Each topicref has a required ref attribute pointing to a node ID. The optional title child serves as a navtitle: a display label for tables of contents that may differ from the referenced node's own title. When absent, tooling resolves the title from the referenced node.

Topicrefs can nest, enabling sub-sections within a reading path. The required attribute (default true) distinguishes mandatory stops from supplementary material that readers can skip without losing the main thread.

org:concept.org:purpose: Understanding why reading maps exist and how they complement the graph structure of a specification.

org:reference.org:purpose: Structural reference for the map, part, and topicref element hierarchy and their attributes.

rel:relation.rel:note: Reading maps add a navigation dimension to the layered architecture without creating a new layer.
The structural reference assumes understanding of the reading map concept.
The newcomer's guide is a concrete instance of the reading map concept, demonstrating the pattern with real content.

spec:clayers.llm:node: Explains why reading maps exist: the spec's graph structure lacks guided entry points. Maps compose nodes into linear sequences with profiling for audience targeting. Part of the organization layer because they classify navigation, not content type.

Reference for map/part/topicref structure: map requires ID and title, contains parts or topicrefs. Topicrefs have ref (node ID), optional navtitle, optional required flag, and support nesting. Parts group topicrefs under a title.
A curated linear reading path through spec nodes for a specific audience. Provides ordering without duplicating content. Defined in the organization layer as org:map elements.
An entry in a reading map pointing to a spec node by ID. Can be required or optional, and can nest for sub-sections. Carries profiling attributes for audience-specific filtering.
Metadata attributes (audience, experience-level, role) on reading map elements. Inclusive by default: absence means "for everyone." Filtering is a tooling concern, not a schema constraint.
spec:clayers.art:exempt: Conceptual documentation describing the reading map pattern. No code implements this concept.
Structural reference for map/part/topicref element hierarchy. Schema definition exists but is not yet implemented.
Vocabulary term defining the reading map concept. No code artifact corresponds to this definition.
Vocabulary term defining topic references. No code artifact corresponds to this definition.
Vocabulary term defining profiling metadata. No code artifact corresponds to this definition.
Navigation structure composing existing nodes into a guided reading path. No code artifact needed.

org:topicref.org:purpose: Deep dive into how xs:any enables extensibility.

art:mapping.art:note: Documentation section/term in reading-maps.xml describing reading map concepts.
Documentation section/term in reading-maps.xml describing reading map concepts.
Documentation section/term in reading-maps.xml describing reading map concepts.
Documentation section/term in reading-maps.xml describing reading map concepts.
Documentation section/term in reading-maps.xml describing reading map concepts.
Documentation section/term in reading-maps.xml describing reading map concepts.
